blurb:
He came in last fall looking like a different dog entirely. Severely emaciated, skin infection, the whole picture of long-term neglect. It took months of careful fostering just to get Morrie healthy enough to be listed. Now he's 18 pounds and making his foster mom laugh every single day.

Morrie is an Italian Greyhound, somewhere around 9 or 10 years old, but the spunk did not get the memo about his age. He's goofy, playful, and apparently hilarious to live with. He gets along great with other dogs and clearly has opinions and a personality to match.

After what was likely years of being overlooked, Morrie is just looking for one person to call his own. He doesn't need a big family or a complicated setup. He needs someone who will let him be a beloved dog for however many good years he's got left. That is a pretty easy ask for the right person.

Rescue Me GA Inc is based in Dunwoody. Start the process by emailing adrienne@rescuemega.org or submitting an application at rmgdogs.org.
scoring_notes:
• Severe neglect to laughing foster home is the strongest redemption arc in this cohort and will drive reader action
• Months of medical recovery before even being listed means he has been in the system longer than any listing date reveals
• Senior small dog with low-maintenance ask and outsized personality is a highly relatable, urgency-building combination

blurb:
There's a moment with Eugene where everything clicks. He's been circling you for a few minutes, closing the gap a little at a time, and then suddenly he's brushing up against your leg and purring like a small engine. That's him saying you're okay. That's him letting you in.

He's seven years old, a domestic shorthair, and shy in a way that's completely understandable once you know his deal. He needs time. Not days, not weeks necessarily, but real time. Months, maybe longer. He's not going to greet you at the door. He's going to watch from across the room and decide for himself. But when he decides you're his person, he'll sit next to you on the couch, crawl into your lap when he's ready, and stick his little tongue out when he's fully, completely relaxed. That's the payoff.

He does best with another animal in the house, a cat or a dog, someone to be near when the world feels like a lot. He's had two tough apartment adoptions that didn't work out, so he really needs a house with enough space to decompress and observe from a distance. If you've got that, and some patience, he's going to be worth every bit of it.

Three Paws Rescue is based in Chamblee. To get started, fill out an adoption application through their Petfinder page or visit pawsumes.com/photos/27377/eugene to see more photos of him.
scoring_notes:
• Two failed adoptions and specific space requirements narrow his pool but create a meaningful challenge for the right reader
• The slow-trust payoff arc is emotionally resonant and will connect with patient, experienced cat owners
• Long shelter history with repeated returns signals urgent need for a permanent match

blurb:
Panda came to rescue through a court case, along with the eight puppies she was raising at the time. The details of the case are unknown, but the scars on her tell enough of the story. All of that is behind her now.

She's about seven years old and fully recovered from heartworm disease, which is no small thing. What the listing makes clear is that she has zero interest in slowing down. She romps and plays with younger dogs in her foster home and holds her own just fine. She does prefer bigger dogs over small breeds, so keep that in mind if you have a little one at home. With people, she's great across the board and apparently has strong feelings about sofa naps, which is very relatable.

Panda has been through a lot and come out the other side as a dog who still wants to play and still wants to trust people. That says a lot about her. If you have a bigger dog at home and a couch she can share, she would probably fit right in.

Rescue Me GA Inc is based in Dunwoody. Email adrienne@rescuemega.org or apply at rmgdogs.org to get started.
scoring_notes:
• Court case origin and visible scars give her story real weight while the sofa-nap detail keeps the tone accessible and warm
• Heartworm recovery plus active play drive home that she has already done the hard work and is ready for a normal life
• A dog who trusts again after documented abuse is a compelling and timely message for readers who need a reason to act now

blurb:
Someone abandoned these two, and they've been quietly adjusting to life at Good Mews ever since. Their names are Elphaba and Glinda, and they came in together, which is the one piece of good luck they've had lately.

They're still figuring out that they're safe. They're not immediately going to run up and greet you at the door. But they have each other, and that's made a difference. They're comfortable together and do well as a pair, which means whoever takes them home gets two cats who already know how to coexist.

They need a patient home. Not a complicated one, just one where the people understand that nervous cats take time and don't push it. Given that, both of them have real potential to open up.

Elphaba and Glinda are bonded and must be adopted together. Good Mews is on Robinson Road in Marietta. Contact them by phone or email to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Bonded pair dynamic—named after Wicked characters—delivers built-in cultural resonance and newsletter headline potential
• Abandoned together and still adjusting signals a longer shelter stay, creating genuine reader urgency to act
• Must-adopt-together requirement narrows the pool significantly, making a public feature their best realistic shot at placement

blurb:
Rudolph came out of a hoarding and backyard breeding situation in South Georgia along with six other dogs, including what they believe is his brother. A year old, poodle mix, and by all accounts a dog who is just now figuring out what it means to actually be a dog.

He was unsure of himself when he arrived, which makes sense given where he came from. But he has blossomed in foster care and his current favorite activity is running full zoomies around the yard with his foster sister Isabelle. He's good with other dogs, good with cats, and already crate trained. He just needs the right setup to keep thriving.

Rudolph does best in a home with at least one other adult dog and a fenced yard. Rescue Me GA also notes that while he's a poodle mix, they can't confirm how much poodle is actually in him, so don't count on him being allergy-friendly. An experienced doodle or poodle owner would be a great match for him.

Rescue Me GA Inc is based in Dunwoody. Email adrienne@rescuemega.org or submit an application at rmgdogs.org.
scoring_notes:
• Hoarding rescue origin with a zoomies-in-the-yard recovery arc is upbeat and shareable for a general newsletter audience
• Young, crate-trained, and good with dogs and cats makes him one of the more broadly placeable candidates in this group
• Arrived with six other dogs from the same situation, suggesting he may be easier to overlook amid a larger intake wave

blurb:
She started her life at Good Mews, got adopted as a kitten, and somehow ended up scared and alone at a county shelter. Nobody knows what happened in between. But Good Mews got the call and brought her straight back, and now Beignet is ready to try again.

She went into a foster home first to decompress, and it didn't take long for her real personality to come out. She loves being petted, likes to eat with company nearby, and when she wants to be close to you, she'll just lean right into you. Not demanding, just present.

One thing to know: she'll occasionally give a small love bite when she gets overstimulated. It's her way of saying she's had enough for now. An adopter who can read her cues will have no problem with it, and honestly, a cat who communicates clearly is a gift.

Good Mews is a cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. Submit an inquiry online or reach out by phone or email to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Full-circle rescue story with emotional pull—returned to her original shelter—gives readers an instant connection
• Communicates clearly and shows affection on her own terms, making her highly relatable to experienced cat owners
• Foster home stint suggests she has been in the system a moderate length of time, adding quiet urgency to her feature

blurb:
After years of living with the same family, Junior ended up at a county shelter with a vague excuse about allergies. He sat there while person after person walked past his kennel. Black dog syndrome is a real thing, and Junior felt every bit of it.

Here's what actually matters about Junior: he has a standing ice cream habit. Bruster's and Pelican's are his regular spots, and he goes home with his favorite volunteer Katie after most outings. He's house trained, well mannered, and completely at ease in a home environment. This isn't a project dog. He already knows how to be a great companion. He just needs someone to choose him.

Junior does best as the only dog in the house and with older kids. If that fits your setup, he's going to be the best decision you've made in a while.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Call or email to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Black dog syndrome, a named favorite volunteer, and a standing ice cream habit combine into one of the most emotionally complete and shareable stories in this batch
• Years with one family followed by repeated kennel pass-overs signals a prolonged wait, giving editors a clear and pressing hook
• Fully house-trained and home-ready with straightforward placement criteria, making reader action feel immediately achievable

blurb:
Jane and her sister Mary have been through a lot. Left behind at an apartment building, bounced through four different foster homes, and still somehow the easiest cats in any room they walk into. The volunteers describe them as zen. That's not a stretch.

Jane is a calico with a coat that sounds impossible on paper: mostly white with black spots and just the faintest trace of orange, barely enough to qualify her as a calico but it counts. She and Mary are genuinely bonded. They came in together and they'd love to go home together. Two calm, low-maintenance cats who already know how to coexist and are just waiting for a quiet place to finally stay.

They do best as the only pets in the home, so no dogs or resident cats. But if you've got a calm household and space for two, these girls will slot right in without drama.

Patty's Kitties holds adoption events at the Pet Supermarket in Toco Hill most Saturdays from 1 to 4pm. That's the easiest way to meet Jane and Mary in person. You can also call or email Patty directly to ask about them.
shelter_hours: Adoption events most Saturdays 1–4pm at Pet Supermarket Toco Hill
scoring_notes:
• Bonded pair requirement reduces the adoption pool but the two-for-one angle is a strong newsletter hook for the right household
• Four foster placements signals a prolonged journey that adds urgency and emotional weight to their story
• Calico with an unusual coat description gives the newsletter a visual detail readers will remember and share

blurb:
Luke's story starts with his mom, Miss Daisy, whose owner passed away unexpectedly. With no family willing to step in, Daisy and her two puppies, Bo and Luke, ended up at the county shelter. They waited weeks for someone to come. Nobody did. Our Pal's Place finally pulled them out.

Luke is a young shepherd mix, smaller than you might expect, with a face that leans more Malinois than German Shepherd. He landed at the rescue right in the middle of their summer youth camp, which meant he spent his first weeks getting trail walks and plenty of attention from kids. He's been described as fun-loving and playful, and it shows.

He's young and has some growing up to do, but he's got a good foundation and a lot of heart. If you've been thinking about a shepherd-type dog, Luke is worth a visit.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Reach out by phone or email to meet him.
scoring_notes:
• A grieving owner's dog, a litter of puppies, and a county shelter standoff—Luke's origin story has genuine emotional weight and broad reader appeal
• Summer youth camp socialization is a charming, photogenic detail that positions him as a fun-loving and well-rounded adoption prospect
• Weeks at the county shelter before rescue pull, plus ongoing placement, indicates a meaningful wait that lends reasonable urgency to the feature

blurb:
His name is Pork Tenderloin, and yes, that alone is a reason to go meet him. He came over from a county shelter to give him a better shot at finding the right home, and the right home is a specific thing for this guy.

He's sweet and will absolutely seek you out for attention when he's in the mood. He just wants it on his schedule, not yours. He can get overwhelmed faster than most cats, so loud, chaotic households aren't really his scene. But for someone who gets how cats work and knows how to read when a cat wants space versus company, he's going to be a really rewarding match.

Good Mews describes him as a little misunderstood, which feels accurate. He's not a difficult cat. He's just a cat who needs someone who gets it.

Good Mews is a cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. Reach out by phone or email to start the process and schedule a visit.
scoring_notes:
• The name alone is a guaranteed reader laugh and an instant conversation-starter that boosts newsletter engagement
• Narrower adopter profile—needs a calm, cat-savvy home—limits placement odds and raises the stakes of being featured
• Transfer from a county shelter with no timeline details suggests an uncertain but potentially extended wait for the right match

blurb:
When the Our Pal's Place intake team pulled Otis off a county shelter kill list, they found a dog so overwhelmed by the noise and chaos that he was shaking in his own kennel. The second they got him into a quiet room, he walked over and buried his head in someone's lap. That was all he needed.

At Our Pal's Place, the real Otis showed up fast. Tail wagging, house manners intact, sitting politely for attention. He hadn't forgotten any of it. He was just too stressed to show it before. The volunteers describe him as a fun-loving guy who genuinely loves making the people around him happy.

He's got his footing back now and he's ready to go home with someone who will appreciate what they're getting: a loyal, already-trained dog who just had a terrible few weeks and deserves a soft landing.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Reach out by phone or email to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Pulled from a kill list and immediately sought human comfort—a visually cinematic moment that will resonate deeply with readers
• Already house-trained and well-mannered with no behavioral baggage, making him one of the most ready-to-place dogs in this group
• Rescue appears recent given his quick adjustment arc, but kill-list origins inject high emotional urgency regardless of elapsed time
